coredata.dc:title: Quercetin-derived red emission carbon dots: A multifunctional theranostic nano-agent against Alzheimer’s β-amyloid fibrillogenesis
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Zitong
Dong, Xiaoyan
Sun, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional agents with therapeutic and diagnostic capabilities are imperative to the prevention of Alzheimer's disease (AD), which is considered due to abnormal aggregation and deposition of β-amyloid protein (Aβ) as well as oxidative stress. Herein, quercetin (Que)- and p-phenylenediamine (p-PD)-derived red emission carbon dots (CDs) synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method were designed as a novel theranostic nano-agent for the multi-target treatment of AD. R-CD-75 with an optimized composition exhibited significant inhibition of Aβ aggregation and rapid depolymerization of mature Aβ fibrils (<4 h) at micromolar concentrations (2 and 5 μg/mL, respectively). Moreover, R-CD-75 potently scavenged reactive oxygen species and showed turned-on red fluorescence imaging of Aβ plaques both in vitro and in vivo. In vitro assays proved that R-CD-75 significantly mitigated the Aβ-induced cytotoxicity and enhanced the cultured cell viability from 74.9 % to 98.0 %, while in vivo studies demonstrated that R-CD-75 prolonged the lifespan of AD nematodes by over 50 % (from 13 to 20 d). Compared to the precursors Que and p-PD, R-CD-75 inherited some of their structures and functional groups, such as aromatic structures, phenolic hydroxyl and amino groups, which were considered to interact with Aβ species through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, and π-π stacking, thus contributing to its effectiveness in its theranostic functions. This research has opened a new avenue to the development of potent theranostic agents by designing novel carbon dots.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Disaggregation potential of R-CD-75 toward mature Aβ40 fibrils
R-CD-75 scavenge ROS and protect cells against oxidative stress

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of R-CD-75. (a) Fluorescence spectrum of R-CD-75. (b) Normalized absorption (Abs.), excitation (Ex.), and emission (Em.) spectra of R-CD-75. (c) TEM image (Inset, HRTEM images of R-CD-75 (left) and size distribution of R-CD-75 (right)) (d) High-resolution C1s spectrum. (e) High-resolution N1s spectrum. (f) High-resolution O1s spectrum.

Inhibition ability of R-CD-75 toward Aβ40 aggregation. (a) The effect of R-CD-75 concentration on Aβ40 (25 μM) aggregation determined using ThT fluorescence assay. (b) Zeta potential of Aβ40 monomer, R-CD-75, and co-incubation with R-CD-75 at 0 h. (c) The lag time of Aβ40 fibrillization as a function of inhibitor concentration for Que and different CDs. (d) Comparison of the inhibitory effects of Que and different carbon dots on Aβ40 aggregation. The fluorescence of Aβ40 only was set to 100 %. (e) AFM images and (f) CD spectra of Aβ40 (25 μM) after mixing with R-CD-75 at varying concentrations for 96 h. Scale bar, 2 μm.

Disaggregation potential of R-CD-75 toward mature Aβ40 fibrils. (a) Kinetic curves of of R-CD-75 depolymerizing Aβ40 fibrils (25 μM) by ThT fluorescence assay. (b) Normalized ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 fibrils cultured with various CDs and Que at 37 °C for 4 h. (c) AFM images and (d) CD spectra of Aβ40 fibrils after mixing with R-CD-75 (2–50 μg/mL) for 4 h. Scale bar, 2 μm. (e) The rapid decomposition of Aβ40 fibrils (5 μM) by R-CD-75 measured through ThT fluorescence using stopped-flow spectrometer within 100 s.

Fluorescence image of Aβ40 after incubation with or without R-CD-75 at 37 °C for 96 h. Aβ40 (25 µM) with (a, b) 0 µg/mL, (c, d) 5 µg/mL, (e, f) 10 µg/mL, and (g, h) 20 µg/mL R-CD-75 was observed by ThT (Ex: 470/40 nm, Em: 520 nm, green channel) or R-CD-75 fluorescence (Ex: 535/50 nm, Em: 590 nm, red channel), respectively. Scale bars, 100 µm.

Antioxidant capacity of R-CD-75. (a) •OH, O2 •- and DPPH• scavenging efficiency of different agents as a function of concentration. (b) Fluorescence images of the scavenging effect of R-CD-75 of different concentrations toward Aβ40-induced ROS in SH-SY5Y cells. Scale bar, 100 μm.

(a) Viability of SH-SY5Y cells incubated with varying concentrations of R-CD-75. (b) Detoxification effects of R-CD-75 on the cytotoxicity caused by Aβ40. The final Aβ40 concentration in SH-SY5Y cells was 5 μM. Fluorescence images of (c), (d) CL2006 and (e), (f) N2 nematodes co-treated using ThT and R-CD-75. Fluorescence images of (g) CL2006 and (h) N2 nematodes treated by ThT. Aβ deposition in nematodes is indicated by the white arrows. Fluorescent images of Aβ deposits inhibited in CL2006 nematodes treated by (i) and (j) R-CD-75. Scale bars, 100 μm. (k) Lifespan curves of CL2006/N2 nematodes with/without R-CD-75 treatment. The concentration of R-CD-75 added to the culture system was 50 μg/mL.
(a) Schematic representation of the multifunctional applications of R-CDs. (b) The preparation of R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Quercetin-derived red emission carbon dots: A multifunctional theranostic nano-agent against Alzheimer’s β-amyloid fibrillogenesis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and Key Laboratory of Systems Bioengineering and Frontiers Science Center for Synthetic Biology (Ministry of Education), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and Key Laboratory of Systems Bioengineering and Frontiers Science Center for Synthetic Biology (Ministry of Education), Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and Key Laboratory of Systems Bioengineering and Frontiers Science Center for Synthetic Biology (Ministry of Education), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional agents with therapeutic and diagnostic capabilities are imperative to the prevention of Alzheimer's disease (AD), which is considered due to abnormal aggregation and deposition of β-amyloid protein (Aβ) as well as oxidative stress. Herein, quercetin (Que)- and p-phenylenediamine (p-PD)-derived red emission carbon dots (CDs) synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method were designed as a novel theranostic nano-agent for the multi-target treatment of AD. R-CD-75 with an optimized composition exhibited significant inhibition of Aβ aggregation and rapid depolymerization of mature Aβ fibrils (<4 h) at micromolar concentrations (2 and 5 μg/mL, respectively). Moreover, R-CD-75 potently scavenged reactive oxygen species and showed turned-on red fluorescence imaging of Aβ plaques both in vitro and in vivo. In vitro assays proved that R-CD-75 significantly mitigated the Aβ-induced cytotoxicity and enhanced the cultured cell viability from 74.9 % to 98.0 %, while in vivo studies demonstrated that R-CD-75 prolonged the lifespan of AD nematodes by over 50 % (from 13 to 20 d). Compared to the precursors Que and p-PD, R-CD-75 inherited some of their structures and functional groups, such as aromatic structures, phenolic hydroxyl and amino groups, which were considered to interact with Aβ species through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, and π-π stacking, thus contributing to its effectiveness in its theranostic functions. This research has opened a new avenue to the development of potent theranostic agents by designing novel carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a kind of degenerative neurological disease that progressively impairs cognitive and behavioral functions, posing a significant risk to the health of elderly population [1,2]. One of the pathogenic factors of AD is the accumulation and deposition of β-amyloid protein (Aβ) in the brain tissue of patients, resulting in the generation of toxic Aβ oligomers and fibrils and triggering intracellular oxidative stress in neuroglial cells, which gradually causes neuronal synaptic dysfunction and ultimately culminates in neuronal death [3,4]. In addition, the excess reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the AD brain originate from mitochondrial damage in neurons, activation and overexpression of related oxidative enzymes or the Fenton reaction between transition metal ions and Aβ, which can lead to oxidation and cellular damage and further exacerbate the progression of AD [5,6]. Furthermore, Aβ promotes the elevation of ROS and triggers the onset of oxidative stress as evidenced by various research [6,7]. Therefore, it is of paramount importance for the diagnosis and treatment of AD by developing multifunctional integrated drugs that can simultaneously inhibit Aβ aggregation, depolymerization of Aβ fibrils, image Aβ plaques and scavenge ROS.

Carbon dots (CDs), as one of the highly regarded carbon-based nanomaterials, have emerged as a novel Aβ inhibitor because of their exceptional characteristics, such as excellent fluorescence properties, abundant surface functional groups, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and facile synthesis [8,9]. Compared to fluorescent CDs in the blue or green range, red fluorescent CDs have attracted considerable attention for their ability to deep penetration, reduce biological autofluorescence interference and minimize photodamage to healthy tissue, making them particularly suitable for in vivo applications [9–12]. Some CDs have been demonstrated to facilitate the identification of Aβ monomers, oligomers and fibrils via energy transfer or luminescence resonance energy transfer [13,14]. Furthermore, owing to their size effect and ample surface functional groups, some CDs can efficiently restrict Aβ aggregation by interacting with Aβ through electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and hydrophobic interactions [15,16]. Moreover, some CDs can scavenge ROS due to their hydrogen-donating behaviors and electron transfer to the sp2 carbon core [17,18]. Thus, rationally designed CDs are expected to be integrated drugs with theranostic potential for fighting against Aβ fibrillogenesis. There is evidence that it is possible to design fluorescent CDs with various functions because a synthetic CD can inherit the properties of its precursors [19–21]. In recent years, some CDs with desired properties against Aβ were synthesized by the selection of raw materials to partially preserve the features of the raw materials [22,23], for instance, epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) for its inhibitory effect [24], Congo red for its fluorescent response [25], and selenium for its scavenging of ROS on Aβ aggregation [26,27]. However, it is still challenging to develop CDs of multifunctionality with theranostic potentials targeting Aβ fibrillogenesis.

Quercetin (Que) is a flavonoid, known for its diverse biological and pharmacological properties, including anticancer, antioxidant, cardioprotective, anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects[28,29]. Nevertheless, the unique features of Que pose a challenge for its utilization because of its particular structure and intermolecular interactions, which make it inadequate bioavailability and low water solubility and stability[30]. To broaden the applicability of Que, researchers had employed various measures to boost its efficiency. Han and colleagues had effectively transformed Que molecule with low aqueous solubility into Que nanoparticles (NPs) with high water solubility via pulsed laser ablation[31]. Que NPs were employed to control the assembly of Aβ42 and displayed various functions of hindering the aggregation of Aβ42, destabilizing Aβ42 fibrils and reducing the oxidative stress caused by Aβ42. Subsequently, Qi et al. combined the antioxidant activity of selenium and the inhibitory effect of Que by utilizing Que, Na2SeO3, acacia and polysorbate 80 (P80) to develop Que-loaded selenium nanoparticles coated with acacia and P80, which was found to be effective in inhibiting the aggregation of Aβ42 as well as in exerting antioxidant activity[32]. The following new polymeric Que nanorods and Que-coated gold nanoparticles finally could inhibit hen egg white lysozyme aggregation by up to 50 % at 32 μg/mL and 30 μg/mL, respectively[33]. However, despite numerous attempts to improve the efficacy of the Que molecule for Aβ, the majority of synthesized Que-derived nanomaterials were monofunctional and displayed meagre inhibitory effects on Aβ aggregation and disaggregation capacity at low concentrations.

Hence, motivated by the capability of Que in inhibition of Aβ fibrillization and antioxidant property, we have herein proposed using Que as a carbon source to create a series of Que-derived CDs to boost its performance in the functioning against Aβ and to eliminate its disability of low water solubility. Moreover, to further endow Que-derived CDs with red-fluorescence emission, another heteroatom dopant that can modulate the fluorescence properties of CDs, p-phenylenediamine (p-PD), was selected as a nitrogen source, leading to the success in creating a red-fluorescence emission CDs. The red fluorescent CDs derived from Que and p-PD thus simultaneously achieved the multiple functionalities, including inhibition of Aβ aggregation, depolymerization of Aβ fibrils, fluorescent imaging of Aβ plaques, and scavenging of ROS at low concentrations ( Scheme 1 a).

One-step hydrothermal method employed to synthesis red carbon dots (R-CDs) using Que and p-PD, provides the advantage of continuous and homogeneous heating, resulting in the formation of uniform size distribution of quantum dots (Scheme 1 b). It has been suggested that the first step in the formation of carbon dots from phenol can be potentially the spontaneous oxidation to benzoquinone, subsequently, a nucleophilic reaction occurs between the benzoquinone group and the amine group, which results in the formation of CDs through dehydration, carbonation and covalent crosslinking[34,35]. The Que molecule consists of phenolic structures that can be easily oxidized to a quinoid structure. Furthermore, the p-phenylenediamine molecule offers the dual advantage of a hydrophilic amino functional group and a heteroatomic nitrogen dopant. Thus, in the presence of p-PD, the carboxylic group of Que could intimately react with the amino group of p-PD. Eventually, R-CDs were produced under hydrothermal conditions (200 °C, 8 h). Initially, the reaction mixture consisted of dispersed floating yellow Que powders, which subsequently converted into a homogeneous red-brown solution after the reaction. This phenomenon demonstrates that the issue of insufficient aqueous solubility of the natural molecule Que could be effectively solved by the synthesis of R-CDs. To investigate the effect of the raw material composition on the properties of R-CDs, different R-CDs were synthesized by fixing the concentration of p-PD but varying the concentration of Que. So, the mole ratio of Que changed from 0.05 to 1.5, as listed in Table S1, which gave the synthesis conditions and respective names of the various CDs.

To preliminarily confirm the synthesis of R-CDs, with the R-CD-75 as an example, fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to verify the photoluminescence characteristics of the three CDs (R-CD-75, Que-CD and p-PD-CD). As shown in Fig. 1 a, R-CD-75 exhibited the maximum emission intensity at 610 nm with excitation at 480 nm. However, Que-CD and p-PD-CD displayed maximum peaks at 425 nm and 520 nm upon excitation at 380 nm and 360 nm, respectively (Figs. S1a, b). By comparing the fluorescence properties of R-CD-75 with those of Que-CD and p-PD-CD, we could observe that R-CD-75 co-synthesized from Que and p-PD, showed a significant redshift in emission wavelength and a considerable increase in fluorescence intensity. Since the Que molecule possesses the structure of a phenolic compound, its aromatic ring readily adopts a coplanar structure. Moreover, it has been reported that the introduction of hydroxyl group created oxygen defect[36]. In addition, benzene ring in the p-PD could provide an inherent conjugation, and the amino group could introduce a potentially N-doped and hydrophilic peripheral functional group, which could contribute to a long emission luminescence and desirable aqueous solubility of prepared CDs[11,37].

The fluorescence properties of R-CDs were influenced by modulating the mole ratio of Que to p-PD (Table S1). As illustrated in Fig. S2a, R-CD-5 displayed an excitation-dependent behavior and had three more intense emission peaks at 520, 575 and 610 nm with at an excitation of 360, 440 and 480 nm, respectively. This phenomenon might occur because the excessive p-PD could prevent efficient doping of a fraction of elemental nitrogen when a small amount of Que (2.5 mM) reacted with a large amount of p-PD (50 mM) under the hydrothermal condition in the synthesis of R-CD-5. With the gradual increase of Que content, the fluorescence spectra of the remaining R-CDs (R-CD-25 to −150) show excitation-independent and similar photoluminescent characteristics with a consistent fluorescence center (Fig. 1 a and S2b-S2e), and all of them were excited at 480 nm with the maximum emission at 610 nm. It indicates that an appropriate molar ratio of Que/p-PD was crucial for producing consistent and durable R-CDs with long wavelength. The above fluorescence spectra suggest that R-CDs exhibit desirable fluorescence characteristics through modifying the component content, which may allow for its use as an imaging agent.

Then, the normalized absorption, excitation, and emission of R-CD-75 were recorded and are displayed in Fig. 1 b. It could be observed that the absorption of R-CD-75 exhibited three absorption peaks at 250, 290 and 475 nm. The π-π* transition of multiple conjugated CC bonds in the benzene ring structure was responsible for the absorption peak at 250 nm in the UV region[38]. The absorption peak at 290 nm was observed because of the n-π* transition of CO/CN bond. The broad absorption band within 400–500 nm is due to the surface state of R-CD-75. Furthermore, Fig. 1 b demonstrates the excitation and emission spectra of R-CD-75, which displayed a maximum red emission at 610 nm with an excitation wavelength of 480 nm, consistent with the optimal excitation and emission of the 2D fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1 a. Fig. 1 c provides the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of R-CD-75. The uniformly distributed particles of R-CD-75 with an average particle size of 3.66 ± 0.03 nm (Fig. 1 c inset right), had a typical quasi-spherical morphology. The HRTEM image demonstrates that R-CD-75 exhibited a crystal surface spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1 c inset left). The value of lattice spacing corresponds to the inter-planar spacing of (100), illustrating the nearly defect-free crystalline structure of graphene[23]. The elemental composition and chemical states of R-CDs were further determined using x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full survey of XPS illustrates carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements in six kinds of R-CDs, which displayed three peaks at 284.7, 399.4, and 532.3 eV, respectively (Fig. S3). The component proportions for the six R-CDs are provided in Table S2. The proportion of element N within R-CD-5 was 1.78 %, lower than the other R-CDs (R-CD-25 to −150). This could potentially elucidate the redshift in the fluorescence emission wavelength of the remaining R-CDs as compared to R-CD-5, probably because of a greater proportion of nitrogen doping[39–41]. However, the emission wavelength of the prepared R-CDs (R-CD-25 to −150) remained constant regardless of the change in the molar ratio of Que/p-PD. It is hypothesized that a specific percentage of nitrogen doping content in the obtained R-CDs with varying molar ratios of Que/p-PD has no effect on the emission wavelength of R-CDs, despite a certain change in nitrogen doping content. As depicted in Fig. 1 d, the high resolution C1s XPS spectra of R-CD-75 was divided into four binding energy peaks located at 284.3, 285.1, 286.3, and 287.6 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-N, C-O, and CO/CN, respectively. This suggests the presence of sp2 and sp3 structural domains in R-CD-75[42]. Fig. 1 e, 1 f, S4a and S4b show the high-resolution N1s and O1s spectra of six R-CDs, and Table S3 lists the specific contents for each bond type. The high resolution N1s spectra for six kinds of R-CDs all revealed the presence of N species pyridine N (398.7 eV), amino N (399.5 eV), pyrrole N (400.5 eV), and graphite N (401.9 eV), respectively[43]. The high-resolution O1s spectra of all samples could be separated into three peaks at 531.0, 532.4 and 533.6 eV, representing CO, C-O and OC-O groups[44]. Except for R-CD-5, the content of amino N and C-O group presented a trend that initially increased and then decreased from R-CD-25 to R-CD-150 (Table S3) with R-CD-75 having the highest content of amino N and C-O group. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed to analyze the functional groups present on the surface of R-CDs (Fig. S5). The stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H bonds are observed in the range of 3500–3100 cm−1. The peaks located at 1650, 1620,1510, and 1280–1150 cm−1 were corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO, CN, CC, and C-N[45], respectively. Compared to common cases, the wavenumbers of the two peaks of CO and CN in this study shifted to lower values due to the conjugation effect[43]. Besides, the absorption peak observed at 1010 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of C-O. The stretching vibration of C-H and the out-of-plane bending vibration of C-H in the benzene ring of peaks are located at 2930 and 816 cm−1, respectively. These results demonstrate that the prepared R-CDs contain a conjugated aromatic conformation together with O- and N-containing groups.

Que molecule has been identified as a natural small molecule inhibitor of amyloid aggregation. However, its drawbacks of low water solubility and bioavailability make its application limited. Based on the above analysis, the synthesized R-CDs have addressed the problem of low water solubility of Que. However, if the inhibitory effect of R-CDs on Aβ40 fibrillization has been great improved, which is the major issue to unravel. In situ ThT fluorescence experiments were used to investigate the effects of R-CDs on inhibiting Aβ40 fibrillization. The dynamics of Aβ40 aggregation exhibited a standard sigmoida curve (Figs. S6 and 2a). In a concentration-dependent manner, all the samples inhibited the aggregation of Aβ40. The inhibitory efficiency of R-CDs (from R-CD-5 to R-CD-75) on Aβ40 aggregation enhanced as the molar ratio of Que/p-PD increased, with the most robust inhibition observed with R-CD-75. However, the inhibitory effects of R-CD-100 and R-CD-150 diminished by increasing Que content, in particular, the effects were quite weak at low CDs concentrations (<20 μg/mL). As depicted in Fig. 2 a, R-CD-75 could prevent 99 % of Aβ40 aggregation at a concentration of 50 μg/mL and decrease 79 % of ThT fluorescence intensity at 2 μg/mL, outperforming all other R-CDs, demonstrating the most potent inhibitory effect as compared to any other R-CDs. From the structure and surface functional groups of R-CD-75, four possible explanations for the strong inhibitory effect of R-CD-75 on Aβ40 aggregation are considered. First, a tendency of increasing and then decreasing in amino N content was observed from R-CD-25 to R-CD-150, and R-CD-75 exhibited the highest amino N content (Fig. 1 e, Fig. S4a, Table S3). It has been demonstrated that amino-modified inhibitors could strengthen the electrostatic interaction between the inhibitor and Aβ40, consequently efficiently improving the inhibition on Aβ40 aggregation [46–48]. Second, R-CD-75 possessed the highest content of C-O group (Table S3), resulting in abundant amount of catechol structures derived from Que on its surface [24], which might favor its inhibitory capability. Third, R-CD-75 might specifically bind to the central hydrophobic motif of Aβ40 because its conjugated aromatic structures were reported to have a high affinity for the hydrophobic and aromatic residues in Aβ [15,49]. Additionally, the conjugated aromatic structures of R-CD-75 might allow for its binding to Aβ40 via π-π stacking. Fourth, when R-CD-75 bound to an Aβ40 monomer, the zeta potential of R-CD-75 shifted from −12.38 to −6.12 mV, indicating a distinct electrostatic interaction between the two entities (Fig. 2 b). Thus, the negative charges of R-CD-75 interacted with the positively charged residues in Aβ40 [50]. Therefore, the exclusive structure and plentiful surface functional groups of R-CD-75 made it bind to Aβ through diversified interactions and interfered with the formation of Aβ fibrils[51].

Furthermore, the lag time of various R-CDs was calculated, revealing a trend that R-CD-75 at 2–50 μg/mL reduced the aggregation growth retardation period of Aβ40 from 61.1 to 22 h (Fig. 2 c). This result implies that R-CD-75 accelerated the aggregation nucleation of Aβ40. It has been considered that an increase in nucleation or shorten the lag time may occur due to elevated concentrations at surfaces or borders through various interactions[52,53].

The reduction in the maximum relative fluorescence intensity of ThT was used to determine the inhibition efficiency. The inhibitory effects of R-CD-75 were all stronger than those of other CDs and Que molecules as can be seen in Fig. 2 d. Furthermore, the inhibitory effect of R-CD-75 on the aggregation of another major β-amyloid species, Aβ42, was demonstrated (Fig. S7). The results indicate that R-CD-75 had even stronger inhibitory capability on Aβ42 than on Aβ40, because the fibrillation of Aβ42 was almost completely inhibited in the presence of 5 μg/mL R-CD-75, which was lower than that for Aβ40 (5 μg/mL, Fig. 2). Moreover, a comparison with existing CDs in Table S4 further confirmed the superior quality of R-CD-75. This could be attributed to the incorporation of Que to enhance the inhibitory capacity of R-CD-75, resulting in retaining the active catechol functional group of the precursor Que.

The feasibility of R-CD-75 in inhibiting the fibrillization of Aβ40 was similarly verified through AFM imaging in Fig. 2 e. The Aβ40 monomer accumulates independently to form a dense and elongated fibrils network morphology[54]. As rising the concentration of R-CD-75, the Aβ40 fibrils became shorter and more dispersed in length and formed amorphous aggregates. At a concentration as high as 50 μg/mL, Aβ40 aggregation undergoes substantial conversion into small, irregular aggregates with a spherical shape. R-CD-75 at 50 μg/mL almost completely inhibited Aβ40 fibrillization. Effective inhibition of Aβ40 fibrils aggregate formation by R-CD-75 was demonstrated by AFM experiments.

The transformation of Aβ's secondary structure from uncoiled to β-sheet is a crucial step in Aβ fibrillization. Thus, in order to explore the effect of inhibitors on the secondary conformational transition of Aβ40, circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy is frequently employed. The addition of various concentrations of R-CD-75 to the newly Aβ40 monomers revealed that the negative peak of the Aβ40 monomers at around 196 nm remained unchanged, indicating that the circular dichroism spectrum of the Aβ40 monomers was not significantly altered by the presence of R-CD-75 (Fig. S8). Subsequently, Fig. 2 f illustrates the secondary structural changes of Aβ40 aggregates in the presence of R-CD-75 (2–50 μg/mL). Typical positive and negative peaks at 196 and 215 nm, respectively, were observed with 25 µM Aβ40 monomers after 96 h incubation, which indicated that Aβ40 monomers aggregate to form Aβ40 fibrils with β-sheet structures. However, the intensity of the signal peak at ∼215 nm decreased until it disappeared with increasing the concentration of R-CD-75, suggesting R-CD-75 could inhibit the aggregation of Aβ40 monomers. These results imply that R-CD-75 could modify the original aggregation pathway of Aβ40 monomers, successfully interfering with the conversion of Aβ40 monomers to β-sheet-rich Aβ40 fibrils.

The analysis of the inhibition of Aβ aggregation by the aforementioned inhibitors indicate that the R-CD-75 was the most effective inhibitor against Aβ fibrillization. Therefore, the following experiments will explore R-CD-75 as multifunctional nano-agent against Aβ fibrillization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disaggregation potential of R-CD-75 toward mature Aβ40 fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: One of the effective treatment strategies for AD is the disaggregation of mature Aβ fibrils[55]. Thus, in addition to inhibiting Aβ aggregation as discussed above, further investigations were carried out to examine R-CD-75's capability of depolymerizing mature Aβ fibrils. Firstly, the effects of CDs on the kinetics of the disaggregation of mature Aβ fibrils were investigated by the ThT fluorescence assay. The Aβ40 monomers were cultured at 37 °C for 4 d to produce Aβ fibrils. Afterwards, varying concentrations of inhibitors were co-cultured with 25 μM Aβ fibrils for an additional 4 h. Fig. S9a to S9c illustrate that Aβ40 fibrils’ ThT fluorescence intensity decreased by approximately 63 %, 49 % and 42 % within 4 h when treated with 5 μg/mL of Que-CD, Que and p-PD-CD, respectively. However, 5 μg/mL of p-PD did not reduce the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 fibrils, suggesting a lack of disruption capability at this level (Fig. S9d). In contrast, a concentration of 5 μg/mL of R-CD-75 resulted in a 70 % decrease in ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 fibrils ( Fig. 3 a). As illustrated in Fig. 3 b, R-CD-75 exhibited a greater disaggregation impact in comparison to Que-CD, Que, and p-PD-CD, while also retaining a substantial disruption potential at lower concentrations. The Aβ40 fibrils were rapidly degraded, completed in less than 4 h. The effectiveness of R-CD-75 in rapidly degrading Aβ fibrils was further demonstrated by comparing with existing nanomaterials (Table S5). The ability of varying concentrations of R-CD-75 in disaggregating mature Aβ40 fibrils was further confirmed through AFM images (Fig. 3 c). It was observed that Aβ40 fibrils alone preserved a long and dense protofibril morphology after incubating 4 h. Increasing the concentration of R-CD-75 from 2 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL resulted in Aβ40 fibrils breakage, and at 50 μg/mL no more Aβ40 fibrils were observed, indicating significant disruption of Aβ40 fibrils by R-CD-75. R-CD-75 contains conjugated aromatic structures and various functional groups, such as carboxyl, phenolic hydroxyl, and amino groups, which can interact with Aβ40 fibrils through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, and π-π stacking[22]. Consequently, the intermolecular Aβ40 salt bridges could be ruptured, which causes highly ordered fibrils structures to break down. Furthermore, the circular dichroism spectrum of Aβ40 fibrils exhibited the distinctive positive and the negative peaks at 196 and 215 nm after incubating 4 h at 37 °C, respectively, indicating that the Aβ40 fibrils had maintained their β-sheet secondary structure during the 4 h incubation (Fig. 3 d). The negative signal at 215 nm of Aβ40 fibrils obviously weakened with increasing R-CD-75 concentration, demonstrating that R-CD-75 could effectively disassembly mature Aβ40 fibrils into the lower β-sheet structures of Aβ40 aggregates.

To gain further insight into more details of the disaggregation of mature Aβ fibrils, the fast disaggregation kinetics of Aβ40 fibrils regulated by R-CD-75 was explored by stopped-flow fluorescence spectroscopy. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 e, the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 fibrils treated with R-CD-75 reduced quickly within 20 s and then gradually decreased, suggesting that R-CD-75 disrupted the Aβ40 fibrils over a time scale of seconds. In addition, R-CD-75 at concentrations of 2, 5, and 10 μg/mL effectively destroyed 69 %, 83 %, and 90 % of Aβ fibrils within 100 s, respectively. Subsequently, the AFM was employed to examine the rapid decomposition of Aβ40 fibrils at 0 min by various concentrations of R-CD-75. The concentration of R-CD-75 from 0 μg/mL to 50 μg/mL, the long and dense Aβ40 fibrils rapidly fractured and broke into shorter segments, while the number of fibrils decreased (Fig. S10). Furthermore, the outstanding disaggregation capacity of R-CD-75 was demonstrated through a comparison of AFM images of disassembled Aβ40 fibrils by using R-CD-75, Que, Que-CD, p-PD and p-PD-CD at various time intervals (Fig. S11). Within 5 min of exposure to R-CD-75, Aβ40 fibrils were degraded. Moreover, these fibrils gradually disintegrated during 4 h, indicating that R-CD-75 disrupted the amyloid. By contrast, Que, Que-CD, p-PD and p-PD-CD at the same concentration displayed weaker disintegration and visible long protofibrils. Using Que and p-PD as raw materials to produce nano-sized R-CD-75 created a surface enriched in benzene rings, carboxyl groups and phenolic hydroxyl groups that allowed strong interactions with Aβ40 fibrils, leading to the effective and rapid decomposition of Aβ40 fibrils.

Aβ aggregates constitute a fundamental constituent of amyloid plaques. Measuring the degree of deposition of insoluble Aβ aggregates is crucial for the early detection of AD [25,56]. Red fluorescence is preferable for bioimaging applications compared to blue or green fluorescence due to the susceptibility of the latter to interferences from the sample's autofluorescence [9,56]. Above characterization has demonstrated that R-CD-75 had excellent red fluorescent property, so its utilization as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Aβ was explored. As shown in Fig. S12, there were marked increase in R-CD-75’s the fluorescence emission attached to Aβ40 fibrils (Fig. S12a), and as the concentration of Aβ40 fibrils increased, the intensity at 610 nm increased in a non-linear manner (Fig. S12b). Aβ40 aggregates could bind to the surface or boundary of R-CD-75 through electrostatic, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. The multiple interactions restricted molecular vibrations and rotations, resulting in the increased fluorescence intensity of the R-CD-75/Aβ40 aggregates complexes[57]. Since R-CD-75 shows the turn-on fluorescence responsiveness to fibrils, we further investigated the fluorescence imaging of Aβ40 aggregates in vitro. R-CD-75 did not exhibit any green or red fluorescence on its own under the fluorescence microscope (Fig. S13). After incubating Aβ40 monomers with ThT fluorescent probe at 37 °C for 96 h, numerous green fluorescent spots were observed, implying the formation of Aβ40 aggregates ( Fig. 4 a). The green fluorescent signal of ThT-labelled Aβ40 aggregates decreased as rising the concentration of R-CD-75 because of the inhibitory effect of R-CD-75 (Fig. 4 c, e and g), confirming the inhibition findings mentioned above. Interestingly, Fig. 4 d, f and h identified red fluorescent plaques with a distribution consistent with green fluorescent plaques in Fig. 4 c, e and g, respectively. These fluorescence images suggest that R-CD-75 could prevent Aβ40 fibrillization and emit unique red fluorescence by linking to Aβ40 aggregates. The red fluorescence of R-CD-75 made it more suitable for in vivo imaging than the green ThT probe. Additionally, the inhibitory and depolymerizing effects of R-CD-75 implied the strong interactions with Aβ40 aggregates, making it function for localizing and diagnosing Aβ40 aggregates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: R-CD-75 scavenge ROS and protect cells against oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: Toxic aggregates formed during the process of amyloid formation have the potential to stimulate the production of ROS and create cellular oxidative stress, which could exacerbate the disease[5]. Therefore, the removal of ROS is considered a promising therapeutic approach for treating the diseases related to protein aggregation[26,58]. R-CD-75 contains numerous functional groups that could provide hydrogen ions as well as electron transfer towards the sp2 carbon core in the CDs[17], which endows R-CD-75 with the ability to scavenge ROS. In vitro experiments analyzed the scavenging activity of R-CD-75 in relation to two free radicals associated with neurodegenerative diseases, •OH and O2 •-, as well as 2,2-diphenyl-1-trinitrophenylhydrazine (DPPH•) to determine the total antioxidant activity. Fig. 5 a shows the scavenging activity of R-CD-75 on •OH, O2 •- and DPPH• at different concentrations. The elimination efficiency toward •OH, O2 •- and DPPH• all increased progressively in a dose-dependent manner in the presence of R-CD-75. R-CD-75 at 50 μg/mL showed good elimination efficiency, with scavenging activities of up to 60 %, 76 % and 80 % for •OH, O2 •- and DPPH•, respectively. Moreover, the elimination efficiency for O2 •- and DPPH• were also approximately 60 % at a low concentration of R-CD-75 (20 μg/mL). Nevertheless, the •OH scavenging activity of R-CD-75 at 20 μg/mL was lower compared to that for O2 •- and DPPH•.

Then, the intracellular scavenging activity of R-CD-75 was further evaluated with Aβ40 acting as a trigger to stimulate excessive free radicals in SH-SY5Y cells. The DCFH-DA probe reacts with intracellular ROS to produce green fluorescent DCF, which is commonly utilized for quantifying intracellular ROS levels[59]. Fig. 5 b demonstrates that upon addition of Aβ40, SH-SY5Y cells exhibited marked green fluorescence, revealing the significant generation of ROS within the cells via Aβ40 induction. Administration of R-CD-75 concomitant with Aβ40 resulted in a decrease in DCF fluorescence intensity, with an observable reduction in green DCF fluorescence in SH-SY5Y cells as the concentration of R-CD-75 increased. The green DCF fluorescence was significantly reduced in SH-SY5Y cells, and 50 μg/mL of R-CD-75 could effectively eliminate all the Aβ40-induced ROS and lessen the oxidative damage inflicted by ROS in SH-SY5Y cells. In addition, Fig. S14 illustrates that the use of the ROS-producing agent ROSup prompted a considerable green fluorescence indication in SH-SY5Y cells, causing an ellipsoidal cell morphology, indicating that a significant amount of ROS was generated and impacted the cellular structure. R-CD-75 could lessen the green fluorescence and reestablish typical cellular morphology, suggesting that R-CD-75 could effectively scavenge intracellular ROS and reduce oxidative damage to cells.

To guarantee the feasibility and safety of in vivo treatment for AD, researching the biocompatibility of R-CD-75 is essential. In view of the good inhibitory efficiency of R-CD-75 against Aβ40 aggregation and their scavenging activity against Aβ40-induced intracellular free radicals, the protective effects of the R-CD-75 against Aβ40-induced cytotoxicity were further investigated to preliminarily assess its therapeutic potential in AD. The effect of R-CD-75 on cell activity was first investigated using MTT cytotoxicity assay with SH-SY5Y cells. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, R-CD-75 demonstrated negligible cytotoxicity towards SH-SY5Y cells. The SH-SY5Y cells treated with R-CD-75 (2–50 μg/mL) achieved cell activity in excess of 96 %. However, following induction by Aβ40, the viability of SH-SY5Y cells decreased to 74.9 % (Fig. 6 b). R-CD-75 reduced the toxic impact of Aβ40 in a concentration-dependent manner, resulting in a rise in cell viability to approximately 98 % at 50 μg/mL, suggesting that R-CD-75 possesses significant inhibitor against Aβ40 toxicity.

After confirming effective biocompatibility and inhibitory activity of R-CD-75, we went on to investigate its therapeutic ability to eliminate and image Aβ deposition in C. elegans in vivo. Fig. 6 c, d illustrate the labelling of amyloid deposition plaques in CL2006 nematodes using ThT and R-CD-75, the green and red fluorescent amyloid deposition plaques in the nematode were observed, respectively. However, ThT- and R-CD-75-labelled amyloid plaques were not observed in the wild-type N2 nematodes used as controls (Fig. 6 e, f). The results indicate that R-CD-75 could accurately visualize Aβ plaques in C. elegans. The efficacy of R-CD-75 in inhibiting Aβ accumulation in nematodes was then investigated by analyzing its impact on amyloid plaques through fluorescent staining. As shown in the ThT-stained image in Fig. 6 g, a large number of ThT-labelled amyloid plaques were present in adult CL2006 nematodes without treated with R-CD-75’s administration, exhibiting dispersed bright green fluorescent spots. As a comparison, there was no amyloid plaques in the wild-type N2 nematodes (Fig. 6 h). Furthermore, the inhibitory effect of R-CD-75 on Aβ plaque formation in CL2006 nematodes was demonstrated by the absence of clear fluorescent spots of amyloid plaques after treatment with R-CD-75 (Fig. 6 i). Additionally, Fig. 6 j shows the CL2006 nematode exhibiting red fluorescence, which was indicative of R-CD-75 accumulation in the intestinal lumen. Finally, the effect of R-CD-75 on the lifespan of C. elegans was explored. As illustrated in Fig. 6 k, the lifespan of wild-type N2 nematode was not significantly affected by R-CD-75, demonstrating the material's excellent biocompatibility. In addition, all the CL2006 nematodes were paralyzed and dead during 13 d without feeding R-CD-75. In contrast, CL2006 nematodes treated with R-CD-75 presented a remarkable delay of complete paralysis and mortality to 20 d. These findings indicate that R-CD-75 could act as a scavenger and detection agent for Aβ aggregates in vivo.

In summary, we designed Que- and p-PD- derived red-emission CDs (R-CDs) for multi-target theranostics against AD to realize the inhibition of Aβ aggregation, disaggregation of mature Aβ fibrils, the turned-on red fluorescence imaging of Aβ aggregates as well as scavenging of excess ROS. The obtained R-CD-75 with delicately controlled raw material compositions exhibited the most effective inhibitory (2 μg/mL) and rapid disaggregation capabilities (5 μg/mL, <4 h) at low concentrations. In addition, R-CD-75 effectively eliminated intracellular Aβ-induced ROS and protected the neural cell from toxic Aβ40 species and improved cell viability from 74.9 % to 98 %. Furthermore, R-CD-75 could identify Aβ plaques through turning-on red fluorescence imaging of Aβ plaques both in vitro and in vivo and extend the lifespan of CL2006 nematode from 13 d to 20 d. R-CD-75 with inheriting functional residue of Que demonstrated the greatest content of amino N and C-O groups along with a superior ability to inhibit aggregation and decompose mature fibrils. The reactive groups of R-CD-75 such as benzene rings, amino and phenolic hydroxyl groups inherited from the feedstock could interact with Aβ species through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking, leading to the alterations in the Aβ aggregation pathway. Thus, this study offers novel insights into the creation of potent theranostic agents for AD.
